data.title: Tulsa's Route 66 Capital Cruise Sets GUINNESS WORLD RECORDS™ Mark
data.description:
Tulsa's Route 66 Capital Cruise Sets GUINNESS WORLD RECORDS™ Mark

Tulsa's Route 66 Capital Cruise Sets GUINNESS WORLD RECORDS™ Mark

PR Newswire

TULSA, Okla., May 30, 2026

Final count of 3,596 cars is new record for Largest Parade of Classic Cars

TULSA, Okla., May 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- It was a moment that will live on in Tulsa, Route 66 and American history forever.

At approximately 11:52 a.m. on Saturday, May 30, official Guinness World Records adjudicator Michael Empric stepped to the stage and gave the news all of Route 66 was waiting on.

With an official, final count of 3,596 classic cars, Tulsa's Route 66 Capital Cruise: Classic Car Centennial Parade set the GUINNESS WORLD RECORDS™ title, besting the previous mark by 1,105 cars and putting a stamp on the nation's signature Route 66 Centennial celebration.

"I am so proud of Tulsa and so thrilled to set a new world record," said Tulsa Mayor Monroe Nichols. "Tulsa's Route 66 Capital Cruise was a nearly year-long effort from partners all over Tulsa to remind the world that we are the Capital of Route 66® and that no city does things the way Tulsa does. Now, for the next hundred years, whenever anyone mentions the Centennial of Route 66, they'll remember Tulsa, Oklahoma."

Mayor Nichols was one of the Cruise's grand marshals, along with Route 66 historian and author Michael Wallis, Vice Grip Garage founder Derek Bieri, family of the Father of Route 66 Cyrus Avery—led by grandson Bob Berghell—and Oklahoma Lt. Governor Matt Pinnell.

"For years, we have looked to 2026 as a Super Bowl in Oklahoma, and today, Tulsa delivered," said Lt. Governor Pinnell. "Capital Cruise was a massive undertaking, and it paid off in a massive way, nearly doubling the existing world record, proving that Route 66 is not just central to the history of Oklahoma, but the future as well. Congratulations, Tulsa! Here's to the next hundred years of the Mother Road."

In all drivers from 42 states, Canada and France participated in Capital Cruise, bringing more international attention to all corners of Tulsa: north to south, east to west.

"All I can say is, 'Of course we did!' Because Tulsa has never relented its spirit when it sets its mind to a thing," said Tulsa Route 66 Commission Chair, Tulsa City Council Vice Chair and Tulsa City Councilor for District 6 Christian Bengel. "It's who we are. I always believed we would set the record, but never in my own vision of its grandeur and splendor did I imagine Capital Cruise becoming such a shared and anticipated worldwide phenomenon. I said Tulsa had the true moxie to show out, show off, and continue our legacy of being legendary, and I couldn't be prouder of our city. The memory of seeing thousands of classic cars rolling down Route 66 will forever be imprinted on my mind. Congratulations Tulsa, take a bow!"

Cars began leaving Tulsa's Expo Square at 8:00 a.m. The final car reached the adjudicator stage at Mother Road Market at 11:30 a.m. An estimated 100,000 spectators lined 11th Street in Tulsa to take in the unprecedented scene.

"We have been talking about Capital Cruise for almost a full year, and today we definitely got our kicks on Route 66!" said Renee McKenney, senior vice president of tourism for the Tulsa Regional Chamber and president of Tulsa Regional Tourism. "What an amazing effort from everyone across the city to make Capital Cruise a success in the Capital of Route 66®. That community pride drives visitors to Tulsa to experience history and celebrate the Route 66 Centennial together. I am so proud of our city for continuing to make moments that resonate on the world's biggest stage."

Over the course of the route, 16 different Cruise Party Zones turned parking lots into block parties for spectators, making this not just a parade to watch, but a party to participate in. An authentic Route 66-era Oklahoma Highway Patrol car was the final car.

About the Tulsa Route 66 Commission
The Commission supports and assists all ongoing efforts locally and statewide with both public and private entities involved in Route 66 matters. Members also create and help implement specific strategies and plans to encourage economic development and promotion for Route 66. To learn more, visit https://www.facebook.com/Tulsa66Commission/.

About Visit Tulsa
Visit Tulsa's mission is to attract leisure travel visitors and groups to Tulsa and the surrounding area. By highlighting northeast Oklahoma's unique offerings, Visit Tulsa helps bring 9.7 million visitors and $1.4 billion in economic impact annually (2022, per Tourism Economics). Visit Tulsa provides everything a tourist needs to plan and maximize their trip, including attractions, events, dining, and places to stay. For more information, visit visittulsa.com. 

Contact:
Jonathan Huskey
Senior Director of Communications, Tulsa Regional Tourism
jonathanhuskey@visittulsa.com
C: 662.415.1244

TTW Unveils Top 50 Coolcation Travel Destinations in the World for 2026

TTW Unveils Top 50 Coolcation Travel Destinations in the World for 2026

PR Newswire

NEW YORK, May 30, 2026

NEW YORK, May 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Travel And Tour World (TTW) today reveals its definitive guide for the adventurous and the style-savvy: TTW Unveils Top 50 Coolcation Destinations in the World for 2026. This groundbreaking ranking celebrates destinations that combine awe-inspiring landscapes, vibrant cultures, and immersive experiences, setting the gold standard for what it means to travel "cool" in the modern era. Crafted by TTW's editorial experts, the report captures the pulse of a global audience seeking more than vacations—they crave journeys that transform, thrill, and inspire.

Rising global temperatures and increasing heatwave risk are reshaping travel preferences, with more tourists opting for coolcations that offer milder climates and nature-focused experiences. Travelers are increasingly seeking destinations known for glaciers, mountains, forests, fjords, and coastal escapes, where they can enjoy outdoor adventures without extreme heat. From Finland's spectacular Northern Lights and Nepal's towering Himalayan landscapes to Australia's scenic coastlines and Ecuador's dramatic volcanic terrain, these destinations are attracting visitors looking for both comfort and exploration. The trend also reflects growing demand for sustainable tourism, wellness-focused journeys, digital detox experiences, and authentic cultural encounters, redefining travel choices worldwide in 2026.

TTW's 2026 ranking names Switzerland as the top coolcation, followed by Canada, the U.S., Iceland, and Russia. Japan, Norway, the U.K., Mexico, and Germany round out the top ten, blending culture, climate, and adventure. From Finland's northern lights to Nepal's Himalayas, Australia's coasts to Ecuador's volcanic peaks, the top 50 destinations capture the spirit of exhilarating, inspiring, and unforgettable travel—defining what it truly means to travel "cool."  The 2026 coolcation market is booming across Europe, North America, Asia, Oceania, and South America, driven by demand for cooler, immersive travel. Travelers seek glaciers, fjords, mountains, rainforests, and coasts, blending adventure, culture, wellness, and sustainability, with digital detoxes, eco-experiences, and photo-worthy journeys shaping global tourism trends worldwide. 

 "The modern traveler no longer seeks mere destinations—they crave journeys that challenge, inspire, and transform. Coolcations are redefining what it means to explore the world, seamlessly blending high-adrenaline adventure, deep cultural immersion, and thoughtful environmental stewardship.

As El Niño-driven heat risks intensify across many regions, travelers are increasingly embracing cooler-climate destinations, mountain retreats, northern coastal regions, and high-altitude escapes for more comfortable and climate-resilient holidays. These experiences are carefully curated to engage the senses, spark curiosity, and foster meaningful connections with both nature and local communities.

As this movement grows, it signals a profound shift in global tourism, where purposeful exploration, sustainability, and authentic encounters are becoming the benchmarks for truly unforgettable travel." said Anup Kumar Keshan, Founder and Editor-in-Chief of Travel and Tour World.

Detailed Overview

Detailed Overview

1. Switzerland

Switzerland is a quintessential coolcation destination, where snow-capped peaks, crystal-clear lakes, and charming alpine villages meet adventure and luxury. During peak summer, temperatures stay pleasantly cool in alpine regions, typically 10°C–18°C, with crisp, refreshing mountain air. Switzerland is emerging as a premier coolcation destination, especially as rising global temperatures and El Niño-driven weather patterns intensify heat conditions across many regions. 

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Summer (June–September) offers ideal conditions for hiking, cycling, and lake cruises, while winter (December–March) is perfect for skiing, snowboarding, and alpine festivities. Spring and autumn provide quieter landscapes and cultural exploration with fewer crowds. Signature experiences include skiing in Zermatt, paragliding over Interlaken, riding the Glacier Express, wandering Lucerne's medieval streets, visiting Bern's UNESCO-listed Old Town, and sampling chocolate and cheese, blending adventure, culture, and indulgence.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Switzerland blends natural beauty, luxury, and adventure into one seamless package. Its clean cities, scenic villages, and iconic landscapes provide the perfect backdrop for relaxation and exploration, while excellent transportation and hospitality make traveling effortless.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Zermatt offers breathtaking Matterhorn views and a variety of alpine activities including skiing, mountaineering, and cable car adventures. How to reach: Train from Geneva or Zurich.
  2. Interlaken is an adventure hub where visitors can enjoy paragliding, skydiving, lake cruises, and easy access to Jungfraujoch. How to reach: Train from Bern or Zurich.
  3. Lucerne features medieval streets, the iconic Chapel Bridge, the Lion Monument, and scenic lake cruises. How to reach: Train from Zurich or Basel.
  4. Jungfraujoch, known as the "Top of Europe," offers glaciers, snow activities, and panoramic alpine views. How to reach: Cogwheel train from Interlaken Ost.
  5. Geneva is a lakeside city rich in museums, international institutions, and waterfront promenades. How to reach: Flights to Geneva Airport or trains from Lausanne.
  6. Zurich is a cultural hub with excellent shopping, galleries, and vibrant nightlife. How to reach: Flights to Zurich Airport or trains from across Switzerland.
  7. Montreux is a picturesque lakeside town famous for its jazz festival and the historic Château de Chillon. How to reach: Train from Lausanne or Geneva.
  8. Bern, the historic capital, features a UNESCO-listed Old Town, arcades, and a variety of museums. How to reach: Train from Zurich or Basel.
  9. Grindelwald is an alpine village offering hiking trails, skiing, and stunning views of the Eiger. How to reach: Train via Interlaken Ost.
  10. St. Moritz is a luxury ski resort known for wellness retreats, alpine charm, and high-end experiences. How to reach: Train from Zurich or Milan.

2. Canada

Canada is a vast and diverse coolcation destination, where rugged wilderness, pristine lakes, vibrant cities, and rich Indigenous heritage converge. Peak Summer conditions in many northern and coastal areas remain mild, hovering around 8°C–15°C, especially after sunset. Canada is gaining strong momentum as a cool-climate retreat amid rising global temperatures and El Niño-related warming trends, which are making traditional summer destinations hotter and less comfortable. 

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Summer (June–September) is ideal for hiking, kayaking, and city festivals, while winter (December–March) attracts skiers and snowboarders to the Rockies and Quebec. Spring and autumn provide quieter travel, colorful foliage, and wildlife spotting opportunities. Key experiences include exploring Banff and Jasper National Parks, witnessing Niagara Falls, dog sledding in Yukon, whale watching in British Columbia, touring Montreal's historic districts, and sampling poutine and maple treats.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Canada combines vast, unspoiled landscapes with modern cities, offering a rare balance of wilderness and cosmopolitan comfort. Its safety, friendly locals, and accessibility make it an ideal destination for multi-season coolcations, while the mix of outdoor activities and cultural experiences ensures there's something for every traveler.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Banff is famous for its iconic Rocky Mountains scenery, offering hiking, skiing, and the stunning Lake Louise. How to reach: Flights to Calgary, then drive or shuttle.
  2. Jasper provides a wilderness adventure with glaciers, abundant wildlife, and scenic hiking trails. How to reach: Drive from Edmonton or Calgary.
  3. Vancouver is a vibrant coastal city with mountains, beaches, and a rich multicultural atmosphere. How to reach: Flights to Vancouver International Airport.
  4. Toronto is Canada's urban hub, featuring the CN Tower, waterfront attractions, and diverse cuisine. How to reach: Flights to Toronto Pearson Airport.
  5. Montreal offers historic streets, lively festivals, and a renowned culinary scene. How to reach: Flights to Montreal-Trudeau Airport.
  6. Quebec City is a UNESCO-listed destination, celebrated for its cobblestone streets and French charm. How to reach: Flights to Quebec City Airport.
  7. Niagara Falls features spectacular waterfalls with boat tours and observation decks for unforgettable views. How to reach: Drive or train from Toronto or Buffalo.
  8. Whistler is a premier ski resort offering mountain biking, skiing, and a variety of alpine activities. How to reach: Drive or shuttle from Vancouver.
  9. Ottawa is Canada's capital, home to Parliament Hill, museums, and scenic canals. How to reach: Flights to Ottawa Macdonald-Cartier Airport.
  10. Yukon provides Arctic wilderness adventures including dog sledding and opportunities to witness the Northern Lights. How to reach: Flights to Whitehorse Airport.

3. United States

The United States is a dynamic coolcation destination, offering a remarkable blend of iconic cities, breathtaking landscapes, and diverse cultural experiences. In cooler mountain and coastal regions, peak summer temperatures generally range around 12°C–20°C, offering comfortable evenings. The United States offers multiple coolcation zones across its northern and high-altitude regions, attracting travelers escaping rising global heat.

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Spring (March–May) and autumn (September–November) offer mild temperatures and smaller crowds, while summer (June–August) is ideal for beaches, national parks, and outdoor festivals. Winter (December–February) draws skiers to Colorado, Vermont, and the Sierra Nevada. Signature experiences include road-tripping along Route 66, exploring Yellowstone and Grand Canyon National Parks, enjoying Broadway in New York City, wine tasting in Napa Valley, experiencing jazz in New Orleans, and visiting Washington, D.C.'s historic landmarks.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: The United States excels in offering variety: urban sophistication, pristine nature, and a spectrum of climates and cultural experiences. Its well-developed infrastructure, accessibility, and wealth of attractions make it a convenient and endlessly fascinating destination for travelers of all interests.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. New York City features iconic skyscrapers, Broadway shows, world-class museums, and Central Park. How to reach: Flights to JFK, LaGuardia, or Newark Airport.
  2. Grand Canyon offers majestic landscapes with hiking, rafting, and stunning scenic viewpoints. How to reach: Flights to Flagstaff or Phoenix, then drive.
  3. Los Angeles is famous for its beaches, Hollywood attractions, cultural museums, and vibrant nightlife. How to reach: Flights to LAX.
  4. Yellowstone National Park features geysers, diverse wildlife, and numerous hiking adventures. How to reach: Flights to Jackson Hole, Bozeman, or Yellowstone Airport.
  5. Miami is known for its tropical beaches, Art Deco architecture, and lively nightlife. How to reach: Flights to Miami International Airport.
  6. Las Vegas offers world-class casinos, live shows, fine dining, and desert excursions. How to reach: Flights to McCarran International Airport.
  7. Washington, D.C. is rich in monuments, museums, and historical landmarks. How to reach: Flights to Dulles, Reagan, or National Airport.
  8. San Francisco features the Golden Gate Bridge, Alcatraz Island, and distinctive cultural neighborhoods. How to reach: Flights to San Francisco International Airport.
  9. Chicago is renowned for its architecture tours, museums, lakefront activities, and jazz clubs. How to reach: Flights to O'Hare or Midway Airport.
  10. Hawaii (Maui & Oahu) offers beaches, volcanoes, snorkeling, and cultural experiences. How to reach: Flights to Honolulu or Kahului Airport.

4. Iceland

Iceland is a striking coolcation destination, where volcanic landscapes, glaciers, waterfalls, and geothermal hot springs create a surreal natural playground. Even at the peak of summer, the climate remains brisk and fresh at about 7°C–12°C, especially outside urban zones. Iceland stands out as one of the most reliable coolcation destinations globally, especially during periods of rising global temperatures.

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Summer (June–August) offers long daylight hours, mild temperatures, and accessible hiking trails, while winter (December–February) provides prime opportunities to witness the Northern Lights, ice caves, and snow-covered landscapes. Spring and autumn are quieter, ideal for scenic drives and cultural experiences with fewer tourists. The Signature experiences include bathing in the Blue Lagoon, exploring the Golden Circle (Thingvellir, Gullfoss, and Geysir), hiking glaciers, visiting the black sand beaches at Reynisfjara, whale watching in Húsavík, and driving Iceland's Ring Road.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Iceland's compact size allows travelers to encounter glaciers, volcanoes, waterfalls, and fjords in a single trip. Its unique geothermal landscapes, accessible wilderness, and high safety standards make it a rare destination where adventure and comfort coexist perfectly.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Reykjavik is Iceland's vibrant capital, offering art, nightlife, and historic landmarks. How to reach: Flights to Keflavik International Airport.
  2. Blue Lagoon is a geothermal spa known for its milky-blue waters and wellness experiences. How to reach: Drive or shuttle from Reykjavik or Keflavik Airport.
  3. Golden Circle includes Thingvellir National Park, Gullfoss waterfall, and the Geysir geothermal area. How to reach: Day tours or self-drive from Reykjavik.
  4. Jökulsárlón Glacier Lagoon features floating icebergs and boat excursions amidst a stunning glacial landscape. How to reach: Drive along the Ring Road from Vik or Hofn.
  5. Akureyri is a northern city with botanical gardens and opportunities for whale watching. How to reach: Flights to Akureyri Airport or drive from Reykjavik.
  6. Vik offers black sand beaches, Reynisdrangar cliffs, and scenic coastal hiking trails. How to reach: Drive along the Ring Road from Reykjavik.
  7. Snaefellsnes Peninsula features volcanoes, lava fields, and charming coastal villages. How to reach: Drive from Reykjavik.
  8. Dettifoss is Europe's most powerful waterfall, renowned for its dramatic scenery. How to reach: Drive from Myvatn or Akureyri.
  9. Landmannalaugar showcases colorful rhyolite mountains and geothermal hot springs. How to reach: Accessible by 4x4 from Hella or Selfoss.
  10. Húsavík is considered Iceland's whale watching capital, with a picturesque harbor and tours. How to reach: Drive from Akureyri or via tour from Reykjavik.

5. Russia

Russia is an expansive coolcation destination, offering a mix of imperial history, diverse landscapes, and vibrant cultural experiences. Large northern and central stretches experience relatively cool summer weather, usually around 10°C–18°C during peak summers.

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Summer (June–August) is ideal for city sightseeing, the White Nights festival in St. Petersburg, and exploring Siberia, while winter (December–February) turns the country into a snowy wonderland for ice festivals, skiing, and cultural experiences. Spring and autumn are quieter, with pleasant temperatures for exploring cities and the countryside. Signature experiences include touring the Kremlin and Red Square in Moscow, exploring the Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg, cruising the Volga River, hiking in the Altai Mountains, experiencing the Trans-Siberian Railway, and witnessing the Northern Lights in Murmansk.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Russia blends history, art, and wilderness like no other country. Its iconic architecture, world-class museums, and dramatic landscapes provide a multi-layered travel experience. The sheer scale of the country means every visit can combine city sophistication with remote adventure.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Moscow features Red Square, the Kremlin, the Bolshoi Theatre, and stunning historic architecture. How to reach: Flights to Sheremetyevo, Domodedovo, or Vnukovo Airport.
  2. St. Petersburg is renowned for the Hermitage Museum, Peterhof Palace, scenic canals, and vibrant cultural festivals. How to reach: Flights to Pulkovo Airport or train from Moscow.
  3. Lake Baikal is the world's deepest freshwater lake, offering hiking, boating, and winter ice adventures. How to reach: Flights to Irkutsk, then drive or train.
  4. Sochi is a Black Sea resort city with beaches, ski resorts, and Olympic venues. How to reach: Flights to Sochi International Airport.
  5. Kazan blends Russian and Tatar culture, featuring the Kazan Kremlin and historic mosques. How to reach: Flights to Kazan International Airport or train from Moscow.
  6. Murmansk is an Arctic city ideal for viewing the Northern Lights and experiencing polar adventures. How to reach: Flights to Murmansk Airport.
  7. Kamchatka Peninsula is known for its volcanoes, hot springs, and wildlife tours. How to reach: Flights to Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky Airport.
  8. Vladivostok is a Pacific port city with coastal landscapes, rail heritage, and maritime culture. How to reach: Flights to Vladivostok International Airport.
  9. Altai Mountains offer hiking, trekking, and pristine natural landscapes in a remote wilderness setting. How to reach: Flights to Barnaul or Gorno-Altaysk, then drive.
  10. Veliky Novgorod features medieval architecture, historic churches, and significant cultural heritage. How to reach: Flights to St. Petersburg, then drive or train.

6. Japan

Japan is a captivating coolcation destination, where ancient traditions blend seamlessly with cutting-edge modernity. In highland and northern pockets brings gentle warmth with temperatures around 15°C–22°C during peak summers.

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Spring (March–May) is ideal for cherry blossoms and mild temperatures, while autumn (September–November) offers vibrant foliage and comfortable sightseeing. Summer (June–August) is lively with festivals, though cities can be hot and humid, and winter (December–February) is perfect for skiing and snow festivals in Hokkaido and Nagano. Signature experiences include visiting Kyoto's historic temples, exploring Tokyo's bustling neighborhoods, soaking in an onsen in Hakone, skiing in Hokkaido, witnessing Mount Fuji, strolling through Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park, and enjoying Japanese cuisine from sushi to street snacks.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Japan combines technological sophistication with centuries-old culture and breathtaking natural landscapes. Its safety, cleanliness, efficient transportation, and attention to detail in hospitality make it a seamless travel experience, whether exploring urban centers or remote islands.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Tokyo is a bustling metropolis with towering skyscrapers, extensive shopping districts, historic temples, and vibrant nightlife. How to reach: Flights to Narita or Haneda Airport.
  2. Kyoto is renowned for its historic temples, traditional tea houses, and cultural festivals. How to reach: Shinkansen or flights to Kansai International Airport.
  3. Osaka offers street food, nightlife, Universal Studios, and a variety of historic landmarks. How to reach: Flights to Kansai International Airport.
  4. Hokkaido features world-class ski resorts, national parks, and famous snow festivals. How to reach: Flights to New Chitose Airport.
  5. Hiroshima is home to Peace Memorial Park, Miyajima Island, and cultural museums. How to reach: Flights to Hiroshima Airport or Shinkansen from Osaka or Tokyo.
  6. Nara is known for its ancient temples, friendly deer, and significant historic sites. How to reach: Train from Kyoto or Osaka.
  7. Okinawa offers tropical beaches, world-class diving, and rich Ryukyu culture. How to reach: Flights to Naha Airport.
  8. Hakone is famous for its hot springs, Lake Ashi cruises, and views of Mount Fuji. How to reach: Train or bus from Tokyo.
  9. Kanazawa features traditional gardens, historic samurai districts, and art museums. How to reach: Train from Tokyo or Osaka.
  10. Nikko is a UNESCO-listed site with shrines, waterfalls, and scenic hiking trails. How to reach: Train from Tokyo.

7. Norway

Norway is a spectacular coolcation destination, famed for its fjords, mountains, and Arctic landscapes. Fjords and northern landscapes stay refreshingly cool, averaging 10°C–17°C in peak summer.
Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Summer (June–August) offers long days and pleasant temperatures, ideal for hiking, fjord cruises, and cycling, while winter (December–March) provides opportunities for skiing, dog sledding, and witnessing the Northern Lights. Spring and autumn are quieter, perfect for scenic drives and cultural experiences with fewer tourists. Signature experiences include cruising the fjords, hiking in the Lofoten Islands, exploring Oslo's museums, skiing in the mountains, witnessing the Midnight Sun, and taking scenic railway journeys like the Flåm Railway.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Norway's dramatic landscapes, safety, and accessibility make it a top choice for nature and adventure travelers. Its mix of outdoor activities, cultural sites, and unspoiled scenery offers a versatile travel experience that is both thrilling and rejuvenating.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Oslo is Norway's modern capital, offering museums, a scenic waterfront, and historic sites. How to reach: Flights to Oslo Gardermoen Airport.
  2. Bergen serves as a gateway to the fjords and features Hanseatic architecture and the scenic Mount Fløyen. How to reach: Flights to Bergen Airport or train from Oslo.
  3. Lofoten Islands boast dramatic mountains, pristine beaches, and traditional Arctic fishing villages. How to reach: Flights to Leknes or Svolvær Airport.
  4. Tromsø is renowned for Northern Lights viewing, Arctic adventures, and cultural experiences. How to reach: Flights to Tromsø Airport.
  5. Geirangerfjord is a UNESCO-listed fjord offering cruises and hiking trails with breathtaking scenery. How to reach: Drive or bus from Ålesund.
  6. Flåm features a scenic railway, fjord cruises, and hiking opportunities in the surrounding mountains. How to reach: Train from Myrdal or drive from Bergen.
  7. Stavanger offers a historic old town, access to Pulpit Rock, and stunning coastal scenery. How to reach: Flights to Stavanger Airport.
  8. Svalbard presents an Arctic wilderness with glaciers, polar wildlife, and unique landscapes. How to reach: Flights to Longyearbyen Airport.
  9. Trondheim is a historic city known for Nidaros Cathedral and charming riverside streets. How to reach: Flights to Trondheim Airport or train from Oslo.
  10. Nordkapp (North Cape) is Europe's northernmost point, featuring dramatic cliff-top views of the Arctic Ocean. How to reach: Drive from Honningsvåg or flights to Alta Airport.

8. United Kingdom

The United Kingdom is a diverse coolcation destination, offering a rich tapestry of history, culture, and scenic landscapes. Coastal breezes keep summer conditions moderate, typically 12°C–18°C during peak summers.

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Late spring (May–June) and early autumn (September–October) offer mild temperatures and fewer crowds, while summer (July–August) is ideal for festivals, sightseeing, and outdoor activities. Winter (December–February) brings cozy experiences, holiday markets, and historic charm. Signature experiences include touring London's palaces and museums, exploring Edinburgh Castle, walking the Scottish Highlands, visiting Stonehenge, enjoying the Lake District's scenery, exploring Cardiff Castle, and experiencing Northern Ireland's Giant's Causeway.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: The UK is unique in offering world-class cities alongside dramatic countryside and coastal landscapes. Its rich history, cultural festivals, and accessible transport network allow travelers to experience a wide variety of attractions within a relatively compact area.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. London features Buckingham Palace, the Tower of London, world-class museums, and West End shows. How to reach: Flights to Heathrow, Gatwick, or London City Airport.
  2. Edinburgh is known for its historic castle, the Royal Mile, vibrant festivals, and scenic views. How to reach: Flights to Edinburgh Airport or train from London.
  3. Scottish Highlands offer mountains, lochs, and extensive hiking adventures in dramatic landscapes. How to reach: Drive or train from Inverness or Edinburgh.
  4. Stonehenge is an ancient stone circle with an informative visitor center. How to reach: Drive or tour from Salisbury or London.
  5. Lake District features picturesque lakes, hiking trails, and charming villages. How to reach: Train or drive from Manchester or Liverpool.
  6. Cardiff boasts medieval castles, a scenic waterfront, and rich cultural attractions. How to reach: Flights to Cardiff Airport or train from London.
  7. Giant's Causeway is a UNESCO-listed site with basalt columns and stunning coastal scenery. How to reach: Drive from Belfast or take a guided tour.
  8. Bath is famous for Roman baths, Georgian architecture, and cultural tours. How to reach: Train or drive from London or Bristol Airport.
  9. York features medieval streets, York Minster, and historic city walls. How to reach: Train from London or Manchester.
  10. Cambridge is known for its historic university, river punting, and cobbled streets. How to reach: Train from London.

9. Mexico

Mexico is a vibrant coolcation destination, celebrated for its rich culture, ancient history, stunning beaches, and diverse landscapes. Elevated regions provide a cooler escape in peak summer, with temperatures around 14°C–20°C.
Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Winter and spring (November–April) offer warm, dry weather ideal for beaches and sightseeing, while summer (May–September) is hotter and wetter, perfect for exploring jungles and attending lively festivals. Autumn (October) provides fewer crowds and scenic landscapes.   1. La Paz features historic streets, the Witches' Market, cable cars, and museums. How to reach: Flights to El Alto International Airport.
  2. Uyuni is famous for the Salar de Uyuni, the train cemetery, and salt flat tours. How to reach: Flights to Uyuni Airport.
  3. Lake Titicaca (Copacabana & Isla del Sol) offers lakeside villages, Inca ruins, and boat tours. How to reach: Drive from La Paz.
  4. Potosí features Cerro Rico mines, colonial architecture, and museums. How to reach: Flights to Potosí Airport or drive from Sucre.
  5. Sucre is known for whitewashed colonial streets, museums, and rich local culture. How to reach: Flights to Juana Azurduy de Padilla Airport.
  6. Santa Cruz de la Sierra is a vibrant city with parks, markets, and urban attractions. How to reach: Flights to Viru Viru International Airport.
  7. Coroico is located in the Yungas region, offering cloud forests and scenic nature trails. How to reach: Drive from La Paz via the Yungas Road.
  8. Samaipata features archaeological sites, mountains, and forested landscapes. How to reach: Drive from Santa Cruz.
  9. Torotoro National Park offers canyons, caves, and dinosaur footprints. How to reach: Drive from Cochabamba or Sucre.
  10. Pampas of the Beni is ideal for wildlife spotting, river excursions, and jungle tours. How to reach: Drive or small plane from Trinidad.

49. Nepal

Nepal is a breathtaking coolcation destination, celebrated for its towering Himalayan peaks, rich cultural heritage, and vibrant trekking opportunities. Himalayan regions stay crisp and refreshing at 10°C–16°C during peak summers

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Spring (March–May) and autumn (September–November) offer ideal conditions for trekking, sightseeing, and mountain views. Summer (June–August) brings monsoon rains, lush greenery, and fewer tourists, while winter (December–February) provides crisp mountain air and snow at higher altitudes. Signature experiences include trekking in the Annapurna or Everest regions, exploring Kathmandu's temples and Durbar Squares, visiting Lumbini, rafting in Pokhara, experiencing festivals, and sampling local cuisine like momo and dal bhat.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Nepal's combination of world-class trekking, spiritual heritage, and Himalayan scenery offers travelers an immersive and transformative experience. The country allows exploration of high-altitude landscapes, ancient temples, and rich local culture within a relatively compact area.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Kathmandu features Durbar Square, Swayambhunath (Monkey Temple), and Pashupatinath Temple. How to reach: Flights to Tribhuvan International Airport.
  2. Pokhara offers scenic lakes, Himalayan mountain views, and adventure sports. How to reach: Flights to Pokhara Airport or drive from Kathmandu.
  3. Everest Base Camp is an iconic trekking route with breathtaking Himalayan views. How to reach: Trek from Lukla Airport.
  4. Chitwan National Park features wildlife safaris, jungle trekking, and rhino spotting. How to reach: Drive or take a short flight from Kathmandu or Pokhara.
  5. Lumbini is the birthplace of Buddha, featuring monasteries and pilgrimage sites. How to reach: Flights to Gautam Buddha International Airport or drive.
  6. Bhaktapur is a medieval city known for its temples and rich cultural heritage. How to reach: Drive from Kathmandu.
  7. Nagarkot offers panoramic mountain views and ideal sunrise spots. How to reach: Drive from Kathmandu.
  8. Annapurna Circuit features trekking routes, stunning mountain scenery, and traditional villages. How to reach: Drive to starting points from Pokhara.
  9. Bandipur is a hilltop town with traditional architecture and scenic landscapes. How to reach: Drive from Pokhara or Kathmandu.
  10. Rara Lake is a remote lake surrounded by mountains, ideal for trekking adventures. How to reach: Flights to Nepalgunj Airport, then drive and trek.

50. Croatia

Croatia is a stunning coolcation destination, renowned for its Adriatic coastline, medieval towns, and natural parks. Inland mountainous areas remain comfortably mild at 14°C–21°C during peak summers.

Best Time to Visit and Signature Experiences: Late spring (May–June) and early autumn (September–October) offer mild weather, fewer crowds, and ideal conditions for sightseeing, island hopping, and outdoor activities. Summer (July–August) is perfect for beaches, sailing, and festivals, while winter (December–February) is quieter for exploring historic towns and inland regions. Signature experiences include exploring Dubrovnik's Old Town, cruising the Dalmatian islands, visiting Diocletian's Palace, hiking Plitvice Lakes, wine tasting in Istria, and enjoying Croatian cuisine.

Why it Stands Out as a Coolcation Destination: Croatia's mix of historic coastal cities, pristine islands, and national parks provides travelers with a versatile and picturesque experience. Its Mediterranean charm, cultural richness, and natural beauty make it a standout European destination.

Top 10 Destinations:

  1. Dubrovnik features the Old Town, city walls, and stunning views of the Adriatic Sea. How to reach: Flights to Dubrovnik Airport.
  2. Split is known for Diocletian's Palace, historic streets, and a scenic coastal promenade. How to reach: Flights to Split Airport.
  3. Hvar offers beautiful beaches, vibrant nightlife, and island charm. How to reach: Ferry from Split.
  4. Plitvice Lakes National Park features waterfalls, lakes, and extensive hiking trails. How to reach: Drive from Zagreb or Zadar.
  5. Korčula is a medieval town with beaches and a rich wine culture. How to reach: Ferry from Split or Dubrovnik.
  6. Zadar features Roman ruins, the Sea Organ, and a historic town center. How to reach: Flights to Zadar Airport.
  7. Rovinj is a coastal town known for art galleries and Venetian architecture. How to reach: Drive from Pula Airport.
  8. Zagreb is the capital city with historic districts, museums, and lively cafes. How to reach: Flights to Zagreb Airport.
  9. Mljet offers a national park, serene lakes, and tranquil island life. How to reach: Ferry from Dubrovnik or Split.
  10. Trogir features a medieval town, UNESCO World Heritage sites, and waterfront charm. How to reach: Drive from Split Airport.

"Coolcation travel is reshaping the tourism landscape, drawing those who crave climate-tailored adventures, deep nature encounters, and rich cultural narratives. These destinations are at the forefront, offering experiences that are both thrilling and meaningful, showcasing a shift toward travel that combines discovery, adventure, and sustainability," said Anup Kumar Keshan, Founder and Editor-in-Chief of Travel and Tour World.

Earlier, Travel And Tour World has published TTW Picks Top 30 Wellness Tourism Destinations in Americas and Caribbean for 2026,  TTW Ranks the Top 50 Wellness Destinations in the World for 2026  TTW Announces Top 30 Eco-Tourism Destinations in the Americas and Caribbean for 2026,  TTW Reveals 50 Best Eco Tourism Destinations in the World for 2026,  TTW Unveils Top 50 Airlines in the World for 2026 TTW Reveals Top 100 Convention Centers in the World for 2026,  Top 20 Safest Travel Destinations in the Americas and Caribbean for 2026, TTW Reveals Top 50 Safest Travel Destinations Around the World for 2026, TTW Picks Best 50 Heritage Travel Destinations in the World for 2026.

Research Methodology

The Top 50 Coolcation Destinations for 2026 were determined through a rigorous, multi-layered framework combining quantitative analysis, qualitative assessment, traveler behavior trends, sustainability measures, and destination performance metrics. Drawing on TTW's editorial expertise, the ranking highlights destinations that blend adventure, cultural immersion, environmental responsibility, and memorable experiences.

As coolcation tourism grows rapidly, destinations were evaluated for their ability to deliver transformative, authentic, and high-adrenaline experiences. The study emphasizes landscapes, adventure infrastructure, cultural authenticity, sustainable practices, and shareable moments.

1. Research Design
The study used descriptive, comparative, and evaluative methods to assess adventure infrastructure, natural and cultural attractions, sustainability, traveler satisfaction, and unique experiences. Priority was given to destinations combining thrilling activities, eco-conscious tourism, and immersive cultural experiences.

2. Data Collection
Sources included international tourism boards, hospitality and adventure travel reports, sustainability studies, travel publications, and traveler reviews. Over 100 destinations across all continents were analyzed, with the final Top 50 selected based on adventure offerings, uniqueness, sustainability, popularity, and TTW reader engagement. Social media trends and demand analytics were incorporated.

3. Data Parameters
Evaluation focused on:

  • Adventure & Activities: Trekking, skiing, diving, surfing, volcano trekking, and other unique adventures.
  • Cultural Immersion & Heritage: Historical landmarks, festivals, indigenous culture, cuisine, and arts.
  • Natural Attractions: Mountains, fjords, glaciers, beaches, rainforests, deserts, and biodiversity.
  • Sustainability Practices: Eco-friendly operations, conservation, renewable energy, and responsible tourism.
  • Luxury & Premium Experiences: High-end hospitality, wellness offerings, curated tours, and exclusive packages.
  • Global Popularity & Demand: Visitor arrivals, social media presence, travel press coverage, and reader feedback.
  • Unique Experiences & Cool Factor: Iconic photography, high-adrenaline activities, digital detox, and transformative experiences.

4. Analytical Approach
Destinations were benchmarked against regional peers using standardized adventure, cultural, and sustainability indicators. Trends include:

  • Europe: Switzerland, Iceland, Norway, Finland, U.K. for alpine adventure, northern lights, fjords, and heritage.
  • North America: U.S., Canada, Mexico for outdoor adventure, urban exploration, and natural wonders.
  • Asia & Oceania: Japan, South Korea, Indonesia, New Zealand, Australia for cultural immersion, trekking, island adventures, and eco-tourism.
  • South America & Caribbean: Brazil, Chile, Ecuador, Peru, Costa Rica, Caribbean islands for rainforest, volcanoes, beaches, and adventure tourism.
  • Africa & Middle East: South Africa, Armenia for wildlife safaris, dramatic landscapes, and cultural heritage.

5. Case Studies
In-depth analysis highlighted exemplary models: Switzerland (alpine adventure, luxury resorts), Canada & U.S. (wilderness and city-adventure balance), Iceland & Norway (geothermal and northern lights tourism), Japan & South Korea (cultural immersion and historic landmarks), Brazil & Chile (rainforest trekking, volcanoes, extreme landscapes).

6. Validation & Reliability
Findings were cross-verified with tourism reports, adventure travel studies, sustainability rankings, government statistics, and traveler reviews. A consistent scoring system ensured transparency, credibility, and balanced regional representation, producing a reliable Top 50 Coolcation Destinations ranking for 2026.

data.title: As Big Tech's power demand surges, data centers bring utilities a huge new profit center
data.description:
By Jurica Dujmovic

The market hasn't fully priced the next logical step for the AI buildout: Big Tech acquiring regulated utilities outright

Lake Tahoe in Calif., seen from Emerald Bay. The region's biggest utility, NV Energy, is leaving the market to focus on AI data-center power demand.

The AI data-center boom is quietly cannibalizing America's power grid.

In California, 49,000 households next to Lake Tahoe are about to learn what it means to be on the wrong side of a power-allocation decision. NV Energy, the Nevada utility that has supplied roughly 75% of Liberty Utilities' electricity for the Tahoe region for decades, announced that it will end the arrangement after May 2027.

NV Energy says the transition reflects its own resource needs. Reporting across multiple outlets puts those resource needs in context: Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) are building data centers around the Tahoe-Reno Industrial Center east of Reno, Nev., and data-center growth now accounts for the dominant share of new load on the regional grid.

Liberty Utilities says customers will not lose power, and NV Energy frames the arrangement as always having been temporary. Both statements can be technically accurate and still miss the point.

Liberty has less than a year to replace 75% of its electricity supply in one of the tightest wholesale power markets in the West. Connecting to California's grid would cost hundreds of millions of dollars. The Sierra Club's Tahoe Area Group is asking regulators to slow the approval process, noting that customers in a high wildfire-risk area deserve more than an expedited regulatory review.

There is a legitimate debate about whether this situation was driven purely by data-center demand or by a decade-old supply agreement running its course. NV Energy insists it will continue providing service while Liberty secures its own supply. But the broader dynamic is not in dispute, as data-center electricity use in Nevada represented roughly 22% of the state's electricity generation in 2024 - a share that Nevada's nonprofit Desert Research Institute projects could reach 35% of forecast generation by 2030, based on NV Energy's own integrated resource plan. In that same plan, about 75% of major new load growth is attributed to data centers, nearly all concentrated in Northern Nevada, on the same transmission system that feeds the Lake Tahoe region.

The moral arithmetic here is not complicated. Communities bear the infrastructure risk and the grid strain of concentrating massive power consumption in a region. The benefits (jobs, tax revenue, AI services) distribute far more broadly than the costs. Ordinary ratepayers do not get seats at the table where supply contracts are written. They get press releases.

The Tahoe situation is local. The forces behind it are not.

Sen. Adam Schiff, a Democrat from California, has introduced the Energy Cost Fairness and Reliability Act of 2026, which would require AI data centers to pay the full cost of power-grid upgrades needed to support their operations. Twenty-seven states are considering similar legislation, with California, Ohio and Utah having enacted comparable laws.

The Trump administration attempted a softer version in March, when Amazon.com (AMZN) Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL) and xAI signed a voluntary Ratepayer Protection Pledge to cover their own energy and infrastructure costs. The pledge is voluntary, sets no specific targets and has no mechanism to verify or enforce compliance. Consumer advocates called it meaningless, unenforceable and nonsense.

The consolidation begins

On May 18, NextEra Energy (NEE) announced plans to acquire Dominion Energy (D) in an all-stock deal valued at $67 billion, one of the largest acquisitions in the U.S. power industry and a transaction that would create the world's largest regulated electric utility by market value. The combined company would serve around 10 million customers across Florida, Virginia, North Carolina and South Carolina, with an enterprise value of roughly $420 billion.

Read: What NextEra and Dominion's giant utility merger means for your electric bill

The rationale is explicit. NextEra CEO John Ketchum said scale matters more than ever, and only a company large enough to build faster and finance more cheaply can realistically satisfy hyperscaler demand. Dominion's Virginia operations are the anchor; Virginia is the world's largest data center market, and Dominion is the utility keeping it all running.

For investors, the deal is also a pricing event. Dominion's stock jumped on the announcement while NextEra's initially fell, a spread that captured both the premium attached to AI-grid exposure and the market's uncertainty about whether NextEra was overpaying to get it. The roughly 23% premium to Dominion's prior share price reflects how far utilities with significant data-center footprints have been repriced in the last two years, from regulated infrastructure earning predictable returns to strategic AI assets bidding for the right to be indispensable.

More mergers of this kind are likely to follow.

More: These stocks could land big profits from filling Big Tech's critical water needs

The supply chain of a new industry

What used to be a vendor relationship is becoming something closer to direct ownership, or at minimum, long-term control over supply that was previously left to market pricing.

The Dominion deal is an extreme version of a pattern that has been accelerating throughout the AI infrastructure build-out. Big Tech started by signing power purchase agreements. It has since moved into direct ownership of generation assets. Google's $4.75 billion acquisition of Intersect and Meta's 20-year power-purchase agreements with Vistra (VST) covering more than 2,600 megawatts from the Perry, Davis-Besse, and Beaver Valley nuclear plants are recent examples.

Based on first-quarter earnings, the four largest hyperscalers, Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft and Meta, are expected to spend close to $725 billion in capital expenditure for 2026, most of it directed at AI infrastructure, data centers, chips and networking equipment - a spending rate with no modern precedent in the technology industry.

Each step in this progression represents further vertical integration of the AI supply chain, from silicon to power generation. What used to be a vendor relationship is becoming something closer to direct ownership, or at a minimum, long-term control over supply that was previously left to market pricing.

The AES (AES) acquisition adds another dimension to the consolidation picture. In March, a consortium led by BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners and EQT (EQT) agreed to acquire AES for an enterprise value of about $33.4 billion. AES holds a 12-gigawatt contract portfolio with Amazon, Microsoft and Alphabet. This is not a tech company buying a utility directly, but it is financial infrastructure capital absorbing a power company already wired into the hyperscaler supply chain. The consolidation is running through every door available.

Read: The 'Munificent 7': Why energy stocks are the best way to play the AI build-out, says former Goldman strategist

The question nobody wants to ask

Data centers need power that is guaranteed. Regulated utilities offer it.

The next logical step in this progression is one the market has not fully priced: Big Tech acquiring regulated utilities outright. The gap between the current arrangement and that outcome is narrowing faster than the political conversation has caught up with.

The structural case is straightforward. Data centers need guaranteed power that is deliverable under agreements that spot markets cannot provide. Regulated utilities offer exactly that, along with transmission access, established regulatory relationships and the rights of way that new entrants cannot easily replicate.

The obstacles are real. Regulated utilities are overseen by state and federal commissions whose mandate is consumer protection, not hyperscaler supply chains. A tech company acquiring a rate-regulated utility would face scrutiny that makes standard antitrust review look routine. The political exposure would be significant, particularly for a sector that is already drawing legislative attention and rattling ratepayers.

NextEra's own acquisition history makes those obstacles concrete. The company has failed to absorb regulated utilities in Texas, Hawaii and South Carolina in recent years: Texas regulators blocked it outright, Hawaii rejected the bid as not in the public interest, and NextEra withdrew its South Carolina bid amid a lobbying investigation. The last time it successfully closed a regulated utility acquisition was Gulf Power in 2019. That track record will give state commissions in Virginia, North Carolina and South Carolina significant material to work with, and the deal is subject to approval from FERC, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission and multiple state regulators before it can proceed.

See: The smart money is plugging into these power-sector stocks - and opening new frontiers for AI-driven growth

Railroad companies eventually owned the land alongside their tracks. The telecom companies eventually owned the cables.

But the history of infrastructure build-outs suggests the line between technology companies and the physical infrastructure they depend on tends to compress over time. Railroad companies eventually owned the land alongside their tracks, and telecom companies eventually owned the cables. The question of whether the next generation of compute infrastructure ends up vertically integrated all the way to the transmission wire is worth asking before the answer becomes obvious.

data.title: Why double-digit earnings growth won't stop the next bear market
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

Spiking S&P 500 profits often signal the final innings of a bull market. History says stocks are on thin ice.

Wall Street is mistaken to insist that the bull market can't be in danger because corporate earnings are growing fast. Blistering earnings growth of the kind that S&P 500 SPX companies have been reporting lately doesn't keep the bears away.

The opposite is more likely: Sky-high earnings growth rates often are seen near the end of bull markets.

FactSet estimates, based on earnings reports from 94% of S&P 500 companies, that the year-over-year growth rate for the S&P 500's EPS will be 28.4% for the first quarter of this year. That's the highest growth rate since the fourth quarter of 2021.

Recall that the 2022 bear market began the next month. Another ominous parallel comes from the top of the internet bubble in March 2000. That month, year-over-year EPS growth was 32.8%.

These are just two data points, but they are consistent with the historical pattern, as you can see in the above chart, which comes from Ned Davis Research. The S&P 500 has produced the best average return in quarters when the index's EPS ranged between 10% and 25% lower than a year prior. Average returns during quarters of greater-than-20% growth were barely positive, and well below average.

This analysis suggests that the S&P 500 will be skating on thin ice over the next few quarters. FactSet says the analyst consensus is for year-over-year earnings growth to be above 20% in each of the next three quarters.

Many investors are surprised by the inverse correlation between growth rates in earnings and the S&P 500. But it makes sense, given that the stock market is forward-looking. At the end of recessions, for example, forward-looking investors will be anticipating the economy's subsequent recovery. So the market will rally off its bear-market lows even as trailing EPS growth rates are awful.

Something similar happens near the end of economic expansions, though in reverse, as the market recognizes that trees don't grow to the sky.

Keep this in mind as you review the table below of valuation indicators. Many bulls object to these indicators' message on the grounds that the market's high levels are supported by robust corporate earnings. That's false comfort, given the analysis presented in this column.

Each of the indicators in the table was chosen because of statistically significant track records in predicting the S&P 500's real total return over the subsequent decade. The three columns on the right side of the chart report where each indicator stands relative to its historical distribution. A 100% reading means the indicator is sending a more bearish signal than 100% of comparable past readings. The average of the readings in the table below is 99%.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

data.title: China's next export shock walks on two legs - and costs less than a used car
data.description:
Tanner Brown

Beijing is funding humanoid robots to slash Chinese factory costs and build a competitive advantage

China is investing tens of billions of dollars to advance its humanoid-robot ambition, enabling Chinese robot manufacturers to grow using state subsidies and market access.

A joint government-business effort could bring not just cheaper Chinese products, but cheaper Chinese production itself.

China's next export shock may not arrive in a shipping container - it may walk onto the factory floor.

For years, the world has worried about China flooding global markets with electric vehicles, solar panels, batteries, steel and consumer electronics. Now, Beijing is trying to industrialize something stranger: humanoid robots that can assemble parts, inspect products, move boxes and eventually take on some of the repetitive work still done by humans.

The investor story is not robot butlers or science-fiction spectacle - it is production costs. If Chinese manufacturers can make robots cheap enough, useful enough and scalable enough, the result could be a new phase in China's manufacturing rise: not just cheaper Chinese products but cheaper Chinese production itself.

Beijing has been unusually explicit about the ambition. China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology said in 2023 that the country should achieve mass production of humanoid robots by 2025 and make them "an important new engine of economic growth" by 2027.

The timing matters. China's workforce is shrinking, wages have risen from the era when the country was simply the world's low-cost workshop, and manufacturers are under pressure from tariffs, weak domestic demand and competition from India, Mexico, Vietnam and elsewhere in Southeast Asia. A cheaper robot worker would not solve all of those problems, but it fits neatly into Beijing's broader answer: to make factories more automated, more productive and harder to displace.

China's public sector is not just talking about humanoid robots; it is spending money to buy them or fund deployments, helping startups get early revenue and proving grounds.

China's public sector is not just talking about humanoid robots; it is spending money to buy them or fund deployments, helping startups get early revenue and proving grounds. Chinese humanoid-robot startups including AgiBot and MagicLab, for example, have received heavy government support recently.

Purchases or purchase orders by government-linked buyers - local governments, state-backed industrial parks, public research institutes, state-owned companies and government-funded demonstration projects - have soared. Such state procurement orders rose to 214 million yuan (roughly $31.5 million) in 2024, up from 4.7 million yuan (around $693,000) just a year earlier.

Some Chinese humanoid robots are being offered for as little as $13,500. That price point is the part investors should notice. A $13,500 robot does not need to be perfect to matter. It only needs to handle enough repetitive tasks in warehouses, electronics plants, automobile factories and logistics centers to change the economics of labor.

A man shakes hands with a humanoid robot from China's Unitree Robotics. The company's G1 robot costs just $13,500.

The humanoid-robot sector resembles the early days of China's now globally competitive electric-vehicle industry.

China already has a head start in automation. The International Federation of Robotics said this month that China's manufacturing sector has an operational stock of about 2 million industrial robots, roughly 4.5 times Japan's level. The IFR also said China's next five-year plan puts robotics at the center of its modern industrial system, though it cautioned that mass adoption of humanoids as universal factory helpers or household assistants is unlikely in the near to medium term.

That caveat matters. Humanoid robots are not about to replace China's factory workforce overnight. The machines still stumble, pause, misread objects and often work best in controlled environments. A recent report from the Mercator Institute for China Studies (MERICS) said China produced nearly 13,000 humanoids in 2025, about 90% of the global total, but noted that the figure was still tiny compared with industrial-robot production. The same report said humanoids in Chinese factories remain "only half as efficient as humans," citing UBtech's (HK:9880) founder.

Still, early does not mean irrelevant. China's electric-vehicle industry also moved from subsidy-dependent experiment to global competitive threat faster than many foreign automobile makers expected. The humanoid-robot sector has some of the same ingredients: government backing, a dense supplier base, brutal domestic competition, large factories willing to experiment and companies racing to cut costs.

Shaking AI to the core

The first winners may be the companies supplying actuators, reducers, sensors, machine vision, batteries, motion-control systems and industrial software.

For some experts, the trend shakes the core of artificial intelligence.

"In the past, AI dealt with information like text and documents. Now, it must move into the real physical world. Fundamentally, this is a paradigm revolution in which AI moves from the virtual to the real," said Ding Han, academician of the Chinese Academy of Sciences and director of the academic committee at Huazhong University of Science and Technology.

The first-order winners may not be the flashiest robot brands. They may be the companies supplying actuators, reducers, sensors, machine vision, batteries, motion-control systems and industrial software. In the same way that EVs created value across batteries, power electronics, charging and materials, humanoid robots could create a new component stack.

The geopolitical backlash is already beginning. U.S. lawmakers introduced a bipartisan bill in March that would ban federal agencies from buying or using humanoid robots made by Chinese companies, citing security risks including data collection and remote control. That reflects growing concern that Chinese robots could become not just cheap hardware but mobile sensors inside sensitive environments.

That is the irony of China's robot push: that, the more successful it becomes, the more likely foreign governments are to restrict it. Yet even restrictions may not stop the broader pressure. If Chinese factories use humanoid robots to lower costs, the effect will show up in exported goods whether or not the robots themselves are sold abroad.

The robots still stumble - but China is teaching them to walk.

More Tanner Brown dispatches:

China's middle class is quietly stockpiling cash - 'shadow saving' - and it could short-circuit the global economy

Yes, consumers in China are consuming again. It's just not their No. 1 priority these days.

Penny pinchers are putting the squeeze on the all-powerful Chinese central government

China's reclusive young 'rat people' stay in bed all day and gnaw away at the country's economic prospects

-Tanner Brown

data.title: America is losing the AI productivity war to 3.5 million Chinese STEM graduates
data.description:
By Shane D. Shook

Big Tech's structural mistakes are costing stock investors and fueling a massive talent crisis

About 40% of China's university degrees are in science, technology, engineering and mathematics - double the U.S.

The United States still leads in private AI investment, but it has not matched that commitment with investment in the human capital needed to sustain it.

The hardest thing to manage is change. I wrote that line more than a decade ago in an article about the "XPocalypse," Microsoft's (MSFT) end-of-life deadline for Windows XP.

My argument then was that the real crisis was not obsolete software. It was the shortage of technically literate professionals capable of guiding organizations through inevitable transitions.

More than a decade later, the names have changed. The lesson has not.

Y2K defined the pattern. The risk was real, but disaster was avoided because skilled people did the work. When nothing happened at midnight as 1999 turned over into 2000, many assumed the threat had been exaggerated - instead of recognizing that it had been successfully managed.

Windows XP became the next version of the same problem. The operating system stayed embedded in retail, banking, healthcare, energy, law enforcement and defense systems long after it should have been retired. The vulnerability was real, but the larger lesson was mostly missed: organizations let technical debt pile up until a deadline turns it into a crisis.

Now we have the "SaaSpocalypse." Headlines warn that agentic AI is breaking the SaaS business model, lowering software valuations and making entire categories of enterprise tools obsolete. Investors are reacting, analysts are talking about "FOBO" - fear of becoming obsolete - and organizations are again asking whether they are ready for what comes next.

AI disruption is real. AI agents can now automate workflows that once required dedicated software tools and teams of human operators. The per-seat pricing model that powered two decades of SaaS economics is under pressure. But the apocalyptic framing misdiagnoses the problem. SaaS is not dying. It is bifurcating.

Platforms requiring precision, auditability, complex state management and regulatory accountability, such as financial systems, healthcare records and compliance infrastructure, will remain essential. What is collapsing is the undifferentiated middle - the horizontal tools that AI agents can replicate cheaply and at scale.

The organizations most exposed are not simply those using the wrong software. They are those that outsourced technical judgment along with technical execution. They purchased SaaS as a substitute for internal capability, accumulated organizational debt - and now lack the human capital to navigate a transition that is fundamentally about people and process.

The old taxonomy still applies: people, process and technology. Technology serves business functions. Processes create efficiency. Qualified people sustain both. But the pace of technological change continues to outrun the education system's ability to produce experienced professionals with current skills.

AI has widened that gap. Data engineers now design orchestration infrastructure that determines whether AI produces value or liability. Security practitioners must govern autonomous agents acting on behalf of enterprises. Business leaders need enough technical fluency to make build-versus-buy decisions in a market changing in real time.

The U.S. is still not producing enough people with STEM skills.

These are not narrow technical tasks. They are the applied outputs of serious STEM education grounded in business context, professional standards and sustained practice. The U.S. is still not producing enough people with these skills.

The numbers are sobering. The U.S. now mints fewer than 820,000 STEM graduates annually, representing about 20% of all degrees awarded. In contrast, China produces roughly 3.57 million STEM graduates each year - about 40% of its university degrees.

At the doctoral level, the gap is even wider. In 2000, the United States awarded 17,830 STEM PhDs, compared with China's 7,520. By 2022, China awarded more than 50,970 STEM doctorates, over 50% more than the 33,820 awarded in the U.S.

This matters directly to AI leadership. Countries building the strongest STEM pipelines today are positioning themselves to define the architecture, governance and standards of AI systems tomorrow.

More than a decade ago, I argued that IT must be treated as a profession, not merely a resource. Finance, medicine, law, engineering and accounting all have formal professional pathways, standards and institutional support. Information technology underpins nearly every critical function of modern society, yet still lacks equivalent professional frameworks.

The technology sector has long harvested talent from a pipeline it does not adequately fund, then wondered why the pipeline runs short.

The AI transition makes this more urgent. As AI absorbs routine execution, the humans left in the loop must be more capable. Their role is shifting from implementation to governance, from configuration to architecture, from maintenance to judgment. That requires better preparation, stronger incentives and professional recognition.

The United States still leads in private AI investment, but it has not matched that commitment with investment in the human capital needed to sustain it. China has embedded AI degree programs across more than 500 universities and integrated corporations directly into research and workforce pipelines. India's AI upskilling surge is driven heavily by corporate sponsorship, with employers treating workforce education as strategic investment. The European Union has committed significant public funding to AI talent development and cross-border STEM mobility.

The U.S. has examples worth scaling. North Carolina's AI Academy at NC State, built with more than 100 corporate partners, combines university credentialing with applied workplace training. North Carolina A&T is partnering with Nvidia (NVDA) and the Office of Naval Research to expand AI and cybersecurity talent. Texas has committed heavily to doctoral research infrastructure through the Texas Institute for Electronics, linking universities, government and industry around semiconductor and defense technology priorities.

These models show what a national strategy should look like: public investment, corporate sponsorship, university research capacity and continuous pathways from undergraduate study through doctoral work. But they remain exceptions. Corporate PhD fellowships from leading technology companies are valuable, but they are filters, not pipelines.

The technology sector has long harvested talent from a pipeline it does not adequately fund, then wondered why the pipeline runs short. That model is no longer sustainable. Federal and state governments must create the policy environment, including tax incentives, credentialing reform, research funding and visa frameworks, that makes corporate STEM investment structurally attractive rather than reputationally optional.

The SaaSpocalypse will pass, as Y2K and the XPocalypse passed, because capable people will do the work. But the underlying shortage will remain.

What I called for in 2014 still stands: STEM education, paired with business, information management and finance, must become sustained national infrastructure. Not as a reaction to this disruption, but as preparation for the next one.

The hardest thing to manage is change. The next is learning from it.

Shane D. Shook is a venture partner at Forgepoint Capital, an early-stage venture capital firm focused on cybersecurity, artificial intelligence and infrastructure software.

data.title: Precigen Presents New Long-Term Durability Data for PAPZIMEOS, Recently Granted Seven-Year Market Exclusivity, Demonstrating Complete Responses Beyond 4 Years
data.description:
Precigen Presents New Long-Term Durability Data for PAPZIMEOS, Recently Granted Seven-Year Market Exclusivity, Demonstrating Complete Responses Beyond 4 Years

Precigen Presents New Long-Term Durability Data for PAPZIMEOS, Recently Granted Seven-Year Market Exclusivity, Demonstrating Complete Responses Beyond 4 Years

PR Newswire

GERMANTOWN, Md., May 30, 2026
  • 15 out of 18 complete responders, or 83%, demonstrated ongoing complete responses for at least 36 months without any additional treatment for RRP
  • 5 complete responders have ongoing responses beyond 4 years
  • Follow-up is ongoing; median duration of complete response has not yet been reached
  • No new adverse safety events have been observed during long-term follow-up
  • US FDA granted seven-year period of orphan drug market exclusivity to PAPZIMEOS

GERMANTOWN, Md., May 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Precigen, Inc. (Nasdaq: PGEN), a commercial-stage biopharmaceutical company specializing in the advancement of innovative precision medicines to improve the lives of patients, today announced updated long-term follow-up data from the pivotal study of PAPZIMEOS™ (zopapogene imadenovec-drba) for the treatment of adults with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP). Updated durability of response data were presented at the 2026 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) Annual Meeting in Chicago in a presentation titled, "Zopapogene imadenovec-drba, a novel non-replicating adenoviral vector-based immunotherapy: Effects on complete and durable responses in recurrent respiratory papillomatosis pivotal trial."

PAPZIMEOS was granted full approval by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and was subsequently granted seven-year market exclusivity, further strengthening its position as the first and only approved therapy for the treatment of adults with RRP.

Key data highlights from the ASCO presentation include:

  • 15 out of 18 complete responders, or 83%, demonstrated ongoing complete responses as of the April 30, 2026 data cutoff (see Figure 1);
  • Patients did not receive any additional treatments for RRP, including surgery or off-label investigational treatments, during this follow-up period;
  • All complete responders had at least 36 months of follow-up, with a median follow-up of 36 months (range: 36 to 51 months) and a mean follow-up of 40 months;
  • 5 complete responders have ongoing responses beyond 4 years;
  • Median duration of complete response has not yet been reached; and
  • No new safety events were observed during long-term follow-up.

"The presentation at ASCO marks an important maturation of the PAPZIMEOS pivotal study data, with all complete responders now followed for at least 36 months, 83% with ongoing response, including 5 patients who are surgery-free beyond 4 years," said Helen Sabzevari, PhD, President and CEO of Precigen. "For adults living with RRP, durability matters. These results continue to show that PAPZIMEOS provides sustained complete responses, reinforcing its role as the new standard of care for a disease historically managed through repeated surgeries."

About RRP
RRP is a rare, debilitating, and potentially life-threatening disease of the upper and lower respiratory tract caused by chronic HPV 6 or HPV 11 infection. RRP can lead to severe voice disturbance, compromised airways, and recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia. Although rare, RRP has the potential for transformation to malignant cancer and can be fatal. Management of RRP has primarily consisted of repeated surgeries, which do not address the underlying cause of the disease and can be associated with significant morbidity as well as significant patient and health system burden. As the number of lifetime surgeries increases, the risk for irreversible iatrogenic laryngeal injury increases with each surgery, and patients may undergo hundreds of these surgeries over their lifetimes. RRP can impact patients' work and social lives, financial stability, and mental health. Patients with RRP can experience substantial impacts to daily living with decreased quality of life and high health care utilization. Based on an internal analysis of claims data and electronic health records, there are approximately 27,000 adult RRP patients in the US.

About PAPZIMEOS™ (zopapogene imadenovec-drba), for subcutaneous injection only
PAPZIMEOS is the first and only FDA-approved therapy for the treatment of adults with RRP and the first and only approved therapy to address the root cause of RRP. PAPZIMEOS is a non-replicating adenoviral vector-based immunotherapy designed to express a fusion antigen comprising selected regions of human papillomavirus (HPV) types 6 and 11 proteins. PAPZIMEOS is designed to generate an immune response directed against HPV 6 and HPV 11 proteins in patients with RRP. Discovered and designed in Precigen's labs using Precigen's proprietary AdenoVerse therapeutic platform, PAPZIMEOS represents a new therapeutic paradigm for RRP.

Indication and Important Safety Information

What is PAPZIMEOS?
PAPZIMEOS is a type of immunotherapy used to treat a condition called recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP) in adults.

What is the most important information I should know about PAPZIMEOS?
Some people may have a reaction to the shot. Signs and symptoms may include redness, pain, swelling, itching, or warmth where the shot was given. After your first treatment, your healthcare provider will watch you for at least 30 minutes to make sure you're feeling okay.

Please contact your doctor immediately if you develop an infection, the reaction to your shot worsens, or you experience any of the below symptoms, which may indicate a systemic allergic reaction:

  • Difficulty breathing
  • Widespread rash
  • Facial swelling

Thrombotic events (blood clots that block your blood vessels) may occur after your PAPZIMEOS shot. Please notify your doctor immediately if you have the following symptoms:

  • Shortness of breath
  • Chest pain
  • Leg swelling
  • Persistent abdominal pain
  • Severe or persistent headaches
  • Blurred vision

What should I know before taking PAPZIMEOS?
Before taking PAPZIMEOS, tell your healthcare provider about all of your medical conditions, including:

  • If you are pregnant or plan to become pregnant because it is not known if PAPZIMEOS will harm the unborn baby.
  • If you are breastfeeding or plan to breastfeed. It is unknown if PAPZIMEOS is present in breast milk, or how it affects the breastfeeding child or milk production. Talk to your healthcare provider about the best way to feed your baby during treatment with PAPZIMEOS.

What are the most common side effects of PAPZIMEOS?
The most common side effects include:

  • Pain, redness, or swelling where the shot was given
  • Feeling tired
  • Chills
  • Fever
  • Muscle aches
  • Nausea (feeling sick)
  • Headache
  • Increased heart rate
  • Diarrhea
  • Vomiting
  • Sweating a lot

These are not all of the possible side effects of PAPZIMEOS. Call your healthcare provider for medical advice about side effects. You are encouraged to report negative side effects of prescription drugs to the FDA. Visit www.fda.gov/medwatch or call 1-800-FDA-1088. You may also report side effects to Precigen, Inc. at 1-855-PGE-NRRP (1-855-743-6777).

Please see full Prescribing Information.

Precigen: Advancing Medicine with Precision®
Precigen (Nasdaq: PGEN) is a commercial-stage biopharmaceutical company specializing in the advancement of innovative precision medicines to address difficult-to-treat diseases with high unmet patient need. Precigen is dedicated to advancing scientific breakthroughs from proof-of-concept through commercialization. With a strong commitment to innovation, Precigen is developing a robust pipeline of differentiated therapies across its core therapeutic areas of immuno-oncology, autoimmune disorders, and infectious diseases. For more information about Precigen, visit www.precigen.com or follow us on LinkedIn or YouTube.
